questions.prompt: Complete the zero conditional: "If you ___ a plant with no water, it dies."
questions.explanation: A zero conditional describes a permanent truth: both clauses use the present simple, so the if-clause is "if you leave". "will leave" and "would leave" belong to the first and second conditionals, and "left" (past) would start a second conditional meaning something imaginary.

questions.prompt: Complete the first conditional: "If she ___ harder, she will get better marks."
questions.explanation: In the first conditional, the if-clause uses the present simple: if she studies. "will study" wrongly puts will in the if-clause, "studied" (past) would make it a second conditional (imaginary), and "would study" also belongs in the result clause only.

questions.prompt: Complete the second conditional: "If he ___ more, he would feel healthier."
questions.explanation: The result "would feel" signals the second (imaginary) conditional, so the if-clause needs the past simple: if he exercised. "exercise" and "exercises" are present forms (zero and first conditional), and "will exercise" wrongly places will in the if-clause.

options.text: If you don't hurry, you will be late.
If she would study, she would pass.
If I had a boat, I would sail around the world.
If you press that button, the machine stops.

questions.explanation: The error is in the second option: would must never appear in the if-clause of a second conditional. The correct form is: If she studied, she would pass. The first option is a correct first conditional, the third a correct second conditional, and the fourth a correct zero conditional.

questions.prompt: Identify the conditional type: "If you drop something heavy on your foot, it hurts."

options.text: First conditional — a real future plan
Zero conditional — a general, always-true fact

questions.explanation: Both clauses are present simple (drop / hurts), and the meaning is an always-true physical fact — that is the zero conditional. The first conditional (a) uses will in the result, the second (b) uses a past if-clause and would in the result, and a mixed conditional (d) combines different time frames.

questions.prompt: Complete the dialogue: "I don't know what to cook tonight." — "___ were you, I'd make pasta."
questions.explanation: The advice phrase is the fixed second conditional "If I were you, I'd…" — here split across the gap, so the answer is "If I" (completing to If I were you). "If I am" uses the present, "If I will be" puts will in the if-clause, and "If I would be" also wrongly places would there.
